abstracts.title: Insurance Requirements
abstracts.desc: Association master policy and unit owner coverage obligations under the Declaration.
abstracts.subtitle: The Association must maintain comprehensive insurance covering the building, common elements, and liability. Unit owners may obtain additional coverage for their own benefit.

blocks.heading: Association Insurance Policies
blocks.text: The Association is required to maintain insurance commencing no later than the first conveyance of a Unit. These policies protect the building structure, common elements, and the Association's leadership and finances.

cards.label: Property Insurance
cards.text: Coverage at 100% actual cash value against all risks of direct physical loss — exclusive of land, excavations, and foundations.

cards.label: General Liability
cards.text: Minimum $1,000,000 covering death, bodily injury, and property damage arising from Common Element use and maintenance.

cards.label: Workers' Comp
cards.text: Required to the extent applicable under Washington law.

cards.label: Fidelity Bonds
cards.text: Covering Board members, Manager, and employees — minimum equal to 3 months' aggregate assessments plus reserves.

cards.label: Association Property
cards.text: Coverage for Association-owned personal property against fire, theft, and other losses.

cards.label: D&O Insurance
cards.text: Directors and officers liability insurance, at the Board's discretion.

sourceText.label: View exact policy language
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Article 13, Insurance
passages: 13.1.1 Property insurance on the Condominium… insuring against all risks of direct physical loss commonly insured against. The total amount of insurance after application of any deductible shall not be less than one hundred percent of the actual cash value of the insured property.

13.1.2 Liability insurance including medical payments insurance, in an amount determined by the Board but not less than One Million Dollars, covering all occurrences commonly insured against for death, bodily injury, and property damage.
13.1.3 Workmen’s compensation insurance to the extent required by applicable laws.
13.1.4 Fidelity bonds… in at least an amount equal to three months aggregate Assessments for all Units plus reserves.
13.1.5 Insurance against loss of personal property of the Association by fire, theft, and other losses.
13.1.6 Such other insurance (including directors and officers liability) as the Board deems advisable.

pullQuote.text: Liability insurance must be not less than $1,000,000, covering all occurrences for death, bodily injury, and property damage arising from Common Elements.
pullQuote.caption: Minimum liability coverage — Article 13.1.2

blocks.heading: Your Insurance
blocks.text: The Association's policy does not prevent you from obtaining insurance for your own benefit. Each Unit Owner is an insured person under the Association's policy with respect to liability arising out of Common Elements or Association membership.
items: You may obtain personal property and liability insurance for your own Unit and belongings.
If there is other insurance in your name covering the same risk, the Association's policy provides primary insurance.
The Association's insurer cannot claim any right of set-off or counterclaim against your personal coverage.

blocks.heading: Key Insurance Provisions
items: Nothing may be done or kept in any Unit or Common Element that increases insurance rates without prior written Board consent.
No Owner may permit anything that would result in cancellation of insurance on any Unit or Common Element.
Each Unit Owner is an insured person under the Association's policy for liability arising from Common Elements.
The insurer waives subrogation rights against the Association, Owners, tenants, and household members.
No act by a Unit Owner will void the Association's policy or prevent recovery — the Association's policy is always primary.

sourceText.label: View exact provisions
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Articles 11 & 13
passages: 11.11: Nothing shall be done or kept in any Unit or in the Common or Limited Common Element which will increase the rate of insurance on the Common Elements or Units without the prior written consent of the Board. No Owner shall permit anything to be done or kept in his Unit or in the Common or Limited Common Elements which will result in the cancelling of insurance on any Unit or any part of the Common or Limited Common Elements.
13.3.1: Provide that each Unit Owner is an insured person under the policy with respect to liability arising out of the Owner’s interest in the Common Elements or membership in the Association.

13.3.2: Provide that the insurer waives its right to subrogation under the policy as to any and all claims against the Association, the Owner of any Unit and/or their respective agents, employees or tenants.
13.3.4: Provide that if, at the time of a loss under the policy, there is other insurance in the name of a Unit Owner covering the same risk, the Association’s policy provides primary insurance.

pullQuote.text: Nothing shall be done or kept in any Unit or Common Element which will increase the rate of insurance or result in cancellation of insurance without prior written consent of the Board.
pullQuote.caption: Insurance protection — Article 11.11

blocks.heading: Damage, Destruction & Reconstruction
blocks.text: When significant damage occurs, the Association has clear obligations and authority for how to respond.
items: The Board must make initial determinations within 30 days of significant damage.
Written notice must be provided to all Owners and first Mortgagees within 30 days.
Significantly damaged portions must be repaired promptly by the Association unless the Condominium is terminated, repair would be illegal, or 80% of Owners vote not to repair.
The cost of repair in excess of insurance proceeds and reserves is a Common Expense.
The Board may authorize an insurance trustee for claims exceeding $50,000.

sourceText.label: View exact reconstruction language
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Article 14, Damage or Destruction
passages: 14.2: In the event of Significant Damage to any part of the Condominium, the Board shall promptly, and in all events within thirty (30) days after the date of Significant Damage… make the following determinations.

14.4.1 Duty to Restore: Any portion of the Condominium for which insurance is required… which is Significantly Damaged shall be Repaired promptly by the Association unless (a) the Condominium is terminated, (b) Repair would be illegal, or (c) eighty percent of the Unit Owners… vote not to repair.

14.5.2: The Board may enter into a written agreement… with any reputable financial institution… that such firm or institution shall act as an insurance trustee to adjust and settle any claim for a loss in excess of Fifty Thousand Dollars ($50,000).

takeaways: The Association carries property insurance at 100% actual cash value plus $1M minimum liability coverage.
Fidelity bonds must cover at least 3 months' aggregate assessments plus reserves.
Don't do anything in your Unit that could increase insurance rates or cause policy cancellation — get Board consent first.
The Association's policy is always primary — your personal coverage is supplemental.
Significant damage triggers a 30-day Board response obligation and mandatory repair unless 80% of Owners vote otherwise.
The Board may appoint an insurance trustee for claims over $50,000.

abstracts.title: Maintenance & Restoration
abstracts.desc: Damage response, reconstruction obligations, and Board authority for repairs.
abstracts.subtitle: How the Association handles damage, destruction, and reconstruction — including Board authority, insurance proceeds, and Owner obligations.

blocks.heading: Your Responsibilities
blocks.text: Unit Owners are responsible for maintaining the interior of their Units. The Board may enter any Unit or Limited Common Element when necessary for maintenance, landscaping, construction, or emergencies.
items: Maintain interior surfaces, fixtures, and systems within your Unit.
Do not obstruct or damage Common or Limited Common Elements.
Nothing may be done in your Unit that increases insurance rates or causes policy cancellation without Board consent.
If you fail to maintain your Unit and it threatens Common Elements or property value, the Board may intervene and charge you.

blocks.heading: Association Authority
blocks.text: The Board has broad authority to maintain, repair, and restore Common Elements and to intervene when Owner neglect threatens the property.
items: Maintain and repair Common Elements and Limited Common Elements.
Enter Units when necessary for maintenance, construction, or emergencies — with as little inconvenience as practicable.
Perform maintenance on a Unit if the Owner fails to do so and it threatens Common Elements or property value.
Levy special charges against a Unit for the cost of any maintenance or repairs performed by the Association.

sourceText.label: View exact Board authority language
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Section 10.4.4
passages: The Board and its agents or employees, may enter any Unit or Limited Common Element when necessary in connection with any maintenance, landscaping or construction for which the Board is responsible or in the event of emergencies. Such entry shall be made with as little inconvenience to the Owners as practicable, and any damage caused thereby shall be repaired by the Board.

10.4(r): Maintain and repair any Unit, its appurtenances and appliances, and any Limited Common Elements if such maintenance or repair is reasonably necessary in the discretion of the Board to protect the Common Element or preserve the appearance and value of the Condominium, and the Owner of said Unit has failed or refused to perform said maintenance or repair within a reasonable time after written notice.

pullQuote.text: The Board may enter any Unit or Limited Common Element when necessary for maintenance or emergencies. Damage caused by entry shall be repaired and paid as a Common Expense.
pullQuote.caption: Board access authority — Section 10.4.4

blocks.heading: Significant Damage Response
blocks.text: When significant damage occurs, the Board must act within 30 days and follow a structured process.

action.label: When significant damage occurs
action.title: Board response timeline
steps: Board assessment within 30 days — determine the extent and nature of the damage.
Written notice to all Owners and Mortgagees — within 30 days of the damage.
Determine repair or termination — the Association must repair unless terminated, illegal, or 80% of Owners vote against.
Hire contractors and manage restoration — the Board has authority to employ architects, attorneys, and contractors.
Appoint insurance trustee if needed — for claims exceeding $50,000.

sourceText.label: View exact damage response language
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Article 14
passages: 14.2: The Board shall promptly, and in all events within thirty (30) days after the date of Significant Damage, make determinations with respect thereto.
14.3: The Board shall promptly, and in all events within thirty (30) days, provide each Owner, and each first Mortgagee with a written notice summarizing the initial Board determination.

14.4.1: Any portion of the Condominium… which is Significantly Damaged shall be Repaired promptly by the Association unless (a) terminated, (b) illegal, or (c) eighty percent of the Unit Owners vote not to repair. The cost of Repair in excess of insurance proceeds and reserves is a Common Expense.

14.5.1: The Board shall have the authority to employ architects and attorneys, advertise for bids, let contracts to contractors and others, and to take such other action as is reasonably necessary to effectuate the Repair and Restoration.

blocks.heading: If Damage Is Not Restored
items: Insurance proceeds for damaged Common Elements are used to restore the area to a compatible condition.
Proceeds for unrepaired Units are distributed to those Owners and lienholders.
Remaining proceeds are distributed to all Owners proportionally by Common Element interest.
If Owners vote not to repair a Unit, its allocated interests are automatically reallocated.

blocks.heading: Emergency Repairs
blocks.text: Even if significant damage will not be fully repaired, the Board retains authority to perform emergency work — including removal of damaged structures, clearing, and grading the property.

pullQuote.text: Even if the Condominium is not to be repaired, the Board may expend insurance proceeds and Association funds for Emergency Work, including removal of damaged buildings and clearing the property.
pullQuote.caption: Emergency authority — Section 14.6

takeaways: The Board must respond to significant damage within 30 days — assessment and written notice to all Owners.
Damaged property must be repaired unless terminated, illegal, or 80% of Owners vote not to repair.
Repair costs beyond insurance proceeds and reserves are a Common Expense shared by all Owners.
The Board may enter your Unit for maintenance or emergencies and charge you if the issue is yours.
If you neglect your Unit and it threatens the property, the Board can fix it and bill you directly.
The Board may appoint an insurance trustee for claims over $50,000.

abstracts.title: ACC Requests
abstracts.desc: Architectural Control Committee approval process for modifications to units and common elements.
abstracts.subtitle: The ACC ensures that all modifications maintain the aesthetic and structural integrity of the condominium, comply with governing documents, and preserve property value.

blocks.heading: What Requires Approval?
blocks.text: All alterations or modifications to a Unit — including structural, exterior, and significant interior changes — require prior written approval from the Board.
items: Structural modifications affecting integrity, mechanical, or electrical systems.
Exterior changes — painting, decorating, or landscaping of entrances, hallways, or limited common elements.
Interior alterations to non-load-bearing partitions, windows, doors, or finished surfaces (must not affect structural components or sprinkler heads).
Common element alterations — construction, alteration, or removal in decks, patios, or storage areas.
Business use within a unit, subject to restrictions on employees and visitor frequency.

sourceText.label: View full requirements from Declaration
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Article 11 & 18
passages: Structural Modifications: Any alterations affecting the structural integrity, mechanical, or electrical systems of a unit or common elements (Article 11, Section 11.5).
Exterior Changes: Modifications to exterior surfaces, including painting, decorating, or landscaping of entrances, hallways, or limited common elements (Article 11, Section 11.10).

Interior Alterations: Changes to interior non-load-bearing partitions, windows, doors, or finished surfaces, provided they do not affect structural components or sprinkler heads (Article 11, Sections 11.5.1, 11.5.2).
Common Element Alterations: Any construction, alteration, or removal in common or limited common elements, such as decks, patios, or storage areas (Article 11, Section 11.15).
Business Use: Operating a private business within a unit, subject to restrictions on employees and visitor frequency (Article 11).
Approval Thresholds: Major changes, such as subdividing or combining units, require approval from 67% of owners and 51% of eligible mortgagees (Article 18, Section 18.2).

pullQuote.text: Major changes such as subdividing or combining units require approval from 67% of owners and 51% of eligible mortgagees.
pullQuote.caption: Major modification threshold — Article 18.2

action.label: Before you start any modification
action.title: ACC approval step by step
steps: Prepare detailed plans — include specifications, materials, and scope of work.
Submit written proposal to the Board — the Board evaluates compliance with the Declaration, Bylaws, and aesthetic standards.
Board review — may require additional provisions to protect other units or common elements.
All Owners notified — the Board notifies all unit owners of proposed changes for transparency.
Approved changes are recorded — amendments to the Declaration or Survey Map are formally recorded.

blocks.heading: Restrictions & Standards
items: Modifications must adhere to standards set by the Board or specific approved plans.
No alterations that compromise structural integrity, mechanical/electrical systems, or building appearance.
Owners must maintain fireplaces and associated equipment (flues, chimneys) as limited common elements per Board rules.
Activities including business operations must not cause excessive noise or disturbance.
Original sprinkler heads must remain uncovered and functional at all times.
Owners may lease units without ACC approval, but lessees must comply with all Declaration and Association rules.

sourceText.label: View full restrictions
sourceText.heading: Declaration — ACC Guidelines
passages: Modifications must adhere to standards set by the Board or specific plans approved in writing (Article 11, Section 11.10).
No alterations that compromise structural integrity, mechanical/electrical systems, or the condominium’s appearance are allowed (Article 11, Section 11.5).
Owners are responsible for maintaining fireplaces and associated equipment (e.g., flues, chimneys) as limited common elements, per Board rules (Article 7, Section 7.4).
Activities, including business operations, must not cause excessive noise or disturbance to other owners (Article 11, Section 11.14).
Original sprinkler heads must remain uncovered and functional (Article 11, Section 11.5.1).
Owners may lease units without ACC approval, but lessees must comply with all Declaration and Association rules (Article 11, Section 11.17).

blocks.heading: Non-Compliance
items: The Board may require owners to perform maintenance or repairs if failure threatens common elements or property value.
Owners are financially liable for costs resulting from violations, gross negligence, or willful misconduct.
Non-compliance may lead to legal action, with the owner responsible for associated costs.
Unpaid assessments or charges for non-compliance may result in a lien against the unit.

sourceText.label: View exact consequences
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Non-Compliance
passages: Board Action: The Board may require owners to perform maintenance or repairs if their failure to do so threatens common elements or the condominium’s value (Article 10, Section 10.4).
Financial Liability: Owners are liable for costs resulting from violations, gross negligence, or willful misconduct affecting limited common elements (Article 11, Section 11.9).
Legal Enforcement: Non-compliance with the Declaration, Bylaws, or rules may lead to legal action, with owners responsible for associated costs (Article 16).
Lien for Assessments: Unpaid assessments or charges for non-compliance may result in a lien against the unit (Article 12, Section 12.2.4).

takeaways: All structural, exterior, and significant interior modifications require written ACC approval.
Major changes (subdividing/combining units) need 67% owner approval and 51% mortgagee consent.
Submit detailed plans and obtain written approval before starting any work.
Non-compliance can result in financial penalties, legal action, and liens against your unit.
Sprinkler heads must remain uncovered and functional — no exceptions.

abstracts.title: Board of Directors
abstracts.desc: Governance structure, elections, authority, and current board composition.
abstracts.subtitle: The Board of Directors governs the Association — managing finances, enforcing rules, overseeing maintenance, and acting on behalf of all Unit Owners.

cards.label: Board Size
cards.text: Three (3) members, a majority of whom must be Unit Owners.

cards.label: Declarant Seat
cards.text: The Declarant retains the right to serve as a full non-voting Board member while Special Declarant Rights remain.

cards.label: Officers
cards.text: President, Vice President, Secretary, Treasurer — elected annually by the Board.

sourceText.label: View governing document language
sourceText.heading: Bylaws — Article 3
passages: 3.1: The affairs of the Association shall initially be governed by a Board composed of at least one (1) but not more than three (3) members as determined by Declarant.

Commencing with the first Association meeting at which the Unit Owners are to elect the entire Board… the Board shall be composed of three (3) Members (not including a Board member designated by Declarant), a majority of whom must be Owners of Units in the Condominium.

The Declarant (or a representative of Declarant) shall have the right to serve as a full non-voting member of the Association Board (with all of the rights and powers of a Board member except for the right to vote).

blocks.heading: How Elections Work
blocks.text: The initial Board has staggered terms — one director for 1 year, one for 2 years, and one for 3 years. After that, each new director is elected to a 3-year term at the annual meeting.
items: Directors serve staggered terms to ensure continuity.
Vacancies (other than removals) are filled by majority vote of remaining Board members until the next annual meeting.

sourceText.label: View exact election rules
sourceText.heading: Bylaws — Section 3.2.1
passages: 3.2.1: The members of the first Board elected entirely by the Unit Owners shall serve terms of office as follows: one (1) director shall serve for a term of one year, one (1) for a term of two years, and (1) for a term of three years. At each annual meeting after the initial Board is elected, the Association members shall elect to a three-year term one new director for each director whose term shall have expired that year.

3.2.2: Vacancies in the Board caused by any reason other than the removal of a Board member by a vote of the Association shall be filled by vote of the majority of the remaining Board members, even though they may constitute less than a quorum.

10.2.4: The Unit Owners, by a two-thirds vote of the voting power in the Association present and entitled to vote at any meeting of the Unit Owners at which a quorum is present, may remove any member of the Board with or without cause.

pullQuote.text: Unit Owners may remove any Board member with or without cause by a two-thirds vote of the voting power present at any meeting where a quorum is present.
pullQuote.caption: Director removal — Section 10.2.4

blocks.heading: Meetings & Decision-Making
items: At least two regular meetings must be held per fiscal year, plus one immediately after the annual Owners' meeting.
Regular meetings require 3 days' notice by mail, telephone, or telegraph.
Special meetings may be called by the President or on written request of at least 2 Board members.
Board members may waive notice in writing, and attendance constitutes waiver.

sourceText.label: View meeting rules
sourceText.heading: Bylaws — Sections 3.2.4–3.2.6
passages: 3.2.4: Regular meetings of the Board… at least two (2) such meetings shall be held during each fiscal year and one (1) such meeting shall be held immediately following the annual meeting of Owners. Notice of regular meetings… at least three (3) days prior.
3.2.5: Special meetings of the Board may be called by the President on three (3) days notice… on the written request of at least two (2) Board members.
3.2.6: Before, at or after any meeting of the Board, any Board member may, in writing, waive notice of such meeting. Attendance by a Board member at any meeting… shall be a waiver of notice.
3.2.7: Each Board member shall receive such sum, as the Owners may from time to time determine, for attendance at a regular or special meeting of the Board.

blocks.heading: Officers of the Board
blocks.text: Officers are elected annually by the Board. Any person may hold two offices concurrently, except President and Secretary cannot be the same person.

cards.label: President
cards.text: Chief executive — presides over all meetings and may appoint committees.

cards.label: Vice President
cards.text: Acts with full authority in the President's absence.

cards.label: Secretary
cards.text: Keeps minutes, maintains records and member lists.

cards.label: Treasurer
cards.text: Manages all funds, receipts, disbursements, and deposits.

blocks.heading: Compensation & Liability
blocks.text: Board members may receive compensation as determined by Owners. No Board member, committee member, or officer acting in good faith and without willful misconduct is personally liable — including for discretionary decisions about lawsuits, investigations, or responses to reports.

blocks.heading: What the Board Can Do
blocks.text: The Board has broad authority but with defined spending limits.
items: Adopt and amend Bylaws, rules, and regulations.
Adopt budgets, impose assessments, and manage finances.
Hire managers, employees, agents, and contractors.
Institute or defend legal proceedings on behalf of the Association.
Regulate use, maintenance, repair, and modification of Common Elements.
Impose late fees and levy fines for violations after notice and hearing.
Enter Units for maintenance, construction, or emergencies.

sourceText.label: View full Board powers
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Section 10.4
passages: (a) Adopt and amend Bylaws, rules, and regulations.
(b) Adopt and amend budgets for revenues, expenditures, and reserves, and impose and collect Assessments.
(c) Hire and discharge or contract with managing agents and other employees.
(d) Institute, defend, or intervene in litigation or administrative proceedings.
(e) Make contracts and incur liabilities.
(f) Regulate the use, maintenance, repair, replacement, and modification of Common Elements.
(k) Impose and collect charges for late payment of assessments and levy reasonable fines.

10.4.2: The Board shall have no authority to acquire and pay for… capital addition or improvement having a total cost in excess of Five Thousand Dollars ($5,000), without first obtaining the affirmative vote of majority of Owners… any expenditure… in excess of Twenty-Five Thousand Dollars ($25,000) must be approved by Owners having not less than sixty-seven percent (67%) of the voting power.

pullQuote.text: Capital improvements over $5,000 require majority Owner approval. Expenditures over $25,000 require 67% Owner approval.
pullQuote.caption: Board spending thresholds — Section 10.4.2

takeaways: Three Board members govern with staggered terms (1, 2, 3 years initially — then 3-year terms).
Directors can be removed by two-thirds vote of voting power present at a quorum meeting.
Officers are elected annually — President and Secretary cannot be the same person.
Capital improvements over $5,000 need Owner approval; over $25,000 need 67% approval.
The Board may enter your Unit for maintenance or emergencies and charge you for the cost.
No personal liability for Board members acting in good faith — even for discretionary decisions.

abstracts.title: Budget & Assessments
abstracts.desc: Annual budget process, reserve funding, assessment obligations, and Owner approval rights.
abstracts.subtitle: How the Association's annual budget is created, approved, and funded — and what happens when costs exceed or fall short of estimates.

blocks.heading: How the Budget Works
blocks.text: Within 60 days before each fiscal year, the Board estimates all charges — Common Expenses, special charges, reserves, expected income, and surplus from the prior year.

action.label: Annual budget cycle
action.title: Budget approval step by step
steps: Board drafts the budget — estimates expenses, reserves, income, and surplus within 60 days before the fiscal year.
Summary sent to all Owners — within 30 days of adopting the proposed budget.
Meeting scheduled — between 14 and 60 days after mailing the summary.
Owners vote — if a majority of voting power rejects the budget, it fails. Otherwise it's ratified (even without a quorum).
If rejected — the last ratified budget continues until Owners approve a new one.

sourceText.label: View exact budget language
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Sections 10.3.3 & 12.1
passages: 12.1: Within sixty (60) days prior to the beginning of each calendar year… the Board shall estimate the charges including Common Expenses, and any special charges for particular Units… shall make provision for creating, funding, and maintaining reasonable reserves for contingencies and operations, as well as for maintenance, repair, replacement and acquisition of Common Elements.

10.3.3: Within thirty days after adoption of any proposed budget… the Board shall provide a summary of the budget to all the Unit Owners and shall set a date for a meeting… not less than fourteen nor more than sixty days after mailing of the summary. Unless at that meeting the Owners of Units to which a majority of the votes in the Association are allocated reject the budget, the budget is ratified, whether or not a quorum is present.

In the event the proposed budget is rejected or the required notice is not given, the periodic budget last ratified by the Unit Owners shall be continued until such time as the Unit Owners ratify a subsequent budget proposed by the Board.

pullQuote.text: Unless Owners holding a majority of votes reject the budget, it is ratified — whether or not a quorum is present.
pullQuote.caption: Budget ratification — Section 10.3.3

blocks.heading: Reserve Fund
blocks.text: The Board must create and maintain a reserve fund from regular monthly assessments for replacement of Common Elements expected to need major repair or replacement during the buildings' useful life.
items: Contributions are calculated so sufficient funds exist to replace or perform major repairs at the end of each element's estimated useful life.
Reserves cover contingencies, operations, maintenance, repair, replacement, and acquisition of Common Elements.
The Board has discretion to adjust reserves based on actual needs and conditions.

blocks.heading: How Assessments Work
blocks.text: Each Owner pays their share of Common Expenses in equal monthly installments on or before the first day of each month.
items: No Owner may exempt themselves from assessments for any reason — including waiver of use or abandonment of their Unit.
If the budget proves inadequate, the Board may levy further assessments at any time.
If the budget proves excessive, the Board may reduce assessments or apply surplus against future obligations.
Special charges may be levied for particular Units when applicable.

sourceText.label: View exact assessment language
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Section 12.2
passages: 12.2: Each Owner shall be obligated to pay its share of Common Expenses and special charges… in equal monthly installments on or before the first day of each month. No Owner may exempt himself from the liability for payment of assessments for any reason including waiver of use or enjoyment of any of the Common Elements or abandonment of the Owner’s Unit.
If the sum estimated and budgeted at any time proves inadequate for any reason (including non-payment for any reason of any Owners Assessment), the Board may at any time levy a further Assessment.
Similarly if the sum estimated and budgeted… proves excessive, the Board may reduce the amount being assessed and/or apply existing funds against future Assessments and/or refund such excess funds.

takeaways: The Board drafts the annual budget within 60 days before each fiscal year.
Owners receive a summary and have 14–60 days to review before the ratification meeting.
The budget passes unless a majority of voting power explicitly rejects it — no quorum needed.
If rejected, the last approved budget stays in effect until a new one is ratified.
You cannot exempt yourself from assessments for any reason, including not using Common Elements.
The Board can adjust assessments up or down based on actual costs vs. estimates.

crossLinks.label: What happens if you don't pay

abstracts.title: Late Fees & Collections
abstracts.desc: Payment deadlines, late penalties, default interest, and rent collection authority.
abstracts.subtitle: What happens when assessments aren't paid on time — including late fees, interest, and the Board's authority to collect rent directly from tenants.

blocks.heading: When Assessments Are Due
blocks.text: Monthly assessments are due on or before the 10th of each calendar month, payable to the Stone Horse Condominium Association. Special assessments are due when indicated by the Board.
items: Checks should be mailed to the Property Manager's office address.
Each Owner pays their share in equal monthly installments.
No Owner may exempt themselves from payment for any reason.

sourceText.label: View exact payment language
sourceText.heading: Rules & Declaration
passages: Monthly assessments are due on or before the tenth day of each calendar month and are payable to the Stone Horse Condominium Association. Special assessments are due when indicated by the Board. Checks should be mailed to the Property Manager’s office address.

12.2: Each Owner shall be obligated to pay its share of Common Expenses… in equal monthly installments on or before the first day of each month. No Owner may exempt himself from the liability for payment of assessments for any reason including waiver of use or enjoyment of any of the Common Elements or abandonment of the Owner’s Unit.

blocks.heading: Late Fees & Interest
blocks.text: If the Property Manager does not receive full payment by the 20th of the month (10 days after the due date), penalties apply immediately.

pullQuote.text: Late fee: 5% of the assessment due. Default interest: 12% per annum on the outstanding balance until paid in full.
pullQuote.caption: Late payment penalties

sourceText.label: View exact penalty language
sourceText.heading: Rules — Late Fee Schedule
passages: If the Property Manager does not receive any assessment in full by the 20th day of the month, or within 10 days of the due date, the Owner will be assessed a late fee of 5% of the assessment due and owing.
In addition, the Owner shall be subject to default interest of 12% per annum of the amount owing until paid in full.
sourceText.ref: Stone Horse Rules — Late Fee Policy

blocks.heading: Board Authority Over Rental Units
blocks.text: If you rent out your Unit and your assessments are more than 30 days overdue, the Board has a powerful collection tool.

pullQuote.text: If assessments are in default over 30 days, the Board may collect rent directly from the tenant. The tenant cannot refuse, and payment to the Association counts as rent paid to the Owner.
pullQuote.caption: Rent collection authority — Section 11.7.4

sourceText.label: View exact rent collection language
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Section 11.7.4
passages: 11.7.4 Rent to Association: If a Unit is Rented by its Owner, the Board may collect, and the tenant or lessee shall pay over to the Board, so much of the rent for such Unit as is required to pay any amounts due the Association hereunder, plus interest costs if the same are in default over thirty (30) days.

The renter or lessee shall not have the right to question payment over to the Board, and such payment will discharge the lessee’s or renter’s duty of payment to the Owner for rent, to the extent such rent is paid to the Association, but will not discharge the liability of the Owner or purchaser and the Unit under this Declaration for assessments and charges.

blocks.text: This does not discharge the Owner's total liability for assessments — it simply redirects rent to cover the debt. The tenant has no right to question the Board's collection.

takeaways: Assessments are due by the 10th of each month. You have until the 20th before penalties kick in.
Late fee is 5% of the amount due, plus 12% annual interest on the outstanding balance.
If you rent your Unit and are 30+ days overdue, the Board can collect rent directly from your tenant.
You cannot exempt yourself from assessments for any reason — even if you don't use Common Elements.
Unpaid amounts may result in liens against your Unit.

abstracts.title: Quick Reference
abstracts.desc: Key numbers, deadlines, and thresholds from all governing documents in one place.
abstracts.subtitle: Every important number, deadline, and threshold from across all Stone Horse governing document abstracts.

cards.label: Assessment Due
cards.text: 10th of each month. Late after the 20th.

cards.label: Damage Response
cards.text: Board must assess and notify Owners within 30 days of significant damage.

cards.label: Budget Summary
cards.text: Sent to Owners within 30 days of Board adoption.

cards.label: Budget Meeting
cards.text: 14–60 days after summary is mailed.

cards.label: Rent Collection
cards.text: Board may collect tenant rent if assessments are 30+ days overdue.

cards.label: Association Meeting Notice
cards.text: 10–60 days before any meeting.

cards.label: Late Fee
cards.text: 5% of the assessment due.

cards.label: Default Interest
cards.text: 12% per annum on outstanding balance.

cards.label: Liability Insurance
cards.text: $1,000,000 minimum coverage.

cards.label: Fidelity Bond
cards.text: 3 months' aggregate assessments plus reserves.

cards.label: Capital Over $5K
cards.text: Requires majority Owner approval.

cards.label: Capital Over $25K
cards.text: Requires 67% Owner approval.

cards.label: Insurance Trustee
cards.text: For claims exceeding $50,000.

cards.label: Remove Director
cards.text: Two-thirds of voting power present at a quorum meeting.

cards.label: Reject Budget
cards.text: Majority of voting power (no quorum needed to ratify).

cards.label: Major Modifications
cards.text: 67% of owners + 51% of eligible mortgagees.

cards.label: Vote Not to Repair
cards.text: 80% of Owners including every affected Owner.

cards.label: Special Meeting
cards.text: Called by president, Board majority, or 20% of Owners.

takeaways: Pay by the 10th — late fee is 5% plus 12% annual interest after the 20th.
The Board must respond to significant damage within 30 days.
Capital spending over $5,000 needs Owner approval; over $25,000 needs 67%.
The Association carries $1M minimum liability and fidelity bonds covering 3 months' assessments.
Directors can be removed by two-thirds vote. Major modifications need 67% + 51% mortgagee consent.
If you rent your Unit and owe 30+ days, the Board can collect rent directly from your tenant.
